focused_output: d1 — Define strength relative to a fixed model M by G1 ≥ G2 iff H(G1,M) ⊆ H(G2,M), where H is the set of admitted histories. Scope, history model, failure model, and client assumptions are null or omitted, so the displayed ordering cannot be established as a universally quantified implication chain.

d2 — PRAM/FIFO and eventual consistency supply a counterexample to totality. PRAM can preserve each writer's order while replicas permanently resolve concurrent, noncommutative writes differently, violating eventual convergence. Eventual consistency can converge after quiescence while transiently exposing one writer's updates out of order, violating PRAM. Therefore neither guarantee universally implies the other.

d3 — Verdict: c1 is refuted. Consistency guarantees form a partial order only after fixing the history model and contract dimensions; convergence, per-writer ordering, causality, real-time ordering, and session guarantees are not one universal scalar axis. A particular bundled contract may yield selected implication edges, but those edges must be stated and proved under its definitions.

Lens sweep — 1 relational: not applicable, no schema or dependencies; 2 transaction: not applicable, no transactional schedule or isolation claim; 3 distributed consistency: fired, using admitted-history inclusion, PRAM/FIFO, and eventual convergence; 4 complexity: not applicable, no algorithm or workload; 5 formal methods: fired, treating each guarantee as a history predicate and refinement as set inclusion; 6 information theory: not applicable, no entropy or encoding question; 7 architecture: not applicable, no component or duplicated-state design is supplied. Empirical closure is not required because the refutation is definitional and countermodel-based.
